subtopic: Microcytic anaemia in a toddler with dietary risk factor
stem: A 22-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his mother because of pallor noticed by the maternal grandmother over the school holidays. He was born at 39 weeks by normal vaginal delivery, weighed 3.4 kg, breastfed until 11 months and was then transitioned to full-cream cow's milk. His mother reports he drinks approximately 900 mL of cow's milk per day from a bottle, including overnight, and is a fussy eater who refuses red meat, eggs and most green vegetables. He passes soft formed stool daily, has no blood in the stool, no recurrent infections, no bruising, and no overseas travel. He is meeting his developmental milestones, walking independently from 13 months, and saying about 20 words. On examination he is alert and active, weight on the 50th centile, height on the 50th centile, conjunctival pallor present, no jaundice, no hepatosplenomegaly and no lymphadenopathy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118 bpm, RR 28, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, soft non-tender abdomen, no organomegaly, no purpura.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 80 g/L, MCV 62 fL, MCH 19 pg, RDW 18%, platelets 480 x10^9/L, WCC 8.2 x10^9/L, ferritin 6 microg/L, CRP <5 mg/L, reticulocytes 1.0%.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Restrict cow's milk to no more than 500 mL per day and start oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day, with FBC and ferritin recheck in 4 weeks
options.rationale: The picture is classic iron deficiency anaemia from excessive cow's milk intake (low ferritin, low MCV, high RDW, mild thrombocytosis). RCH CPG recommends 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron orally once daily and capping cow's milk at 500 mL/day, with response check at 4 weeks (rise in Hb of 10 g/L expected) (RCH CPG Iron deficiency).

options.text: Refer for inpatient packed red cell transfusion given Hb <90 g/L
options.rationale: He is haemodynamically stable and asymptomatic apart from pallor; transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia or Hb <70 g/L with cardiovascular compromise (RCH CPG Anaemia).

options.text: Order haemoglobin electrophoresis before starting any treatment
options.rationale: Ferritin is unequivocally low and the dietary history fits iron deficiency; haemoglobinopathy screening is considered only if iron therapy fails or there is a relevant ethnic/family history (RCH CPG).

options.text: Start IV iron polymaltose infusion as a day-stay procedure
options.rationale: IV iron in a toddler is reserved for malabsorption, intolerance of oral iron, or non-response after a trial of oral therapy; it is not first-line (AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

options.text: Recommend a multivitamin containing 5 mg of elemental iron daily and continue current milk intake
options.rationale: Multivitamin iron content is inadequate for treatment (5 mg total versus a required 3-6 mg/kg/day) and continued high cow's milk intake perpetuates iron deficiency (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia in toddlers is most commonly driven by excessive cow's milk intake (>500-600 mL/day), which displaces iron-rich solids and causes occult GI blood loss. Treatment is dietary modification plus oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day for 3 months total (1 month to correct Hb, then continue to refill stores).
key_points: Cow's milk cap 500 mL/day from 12 months
Oral iron 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental, once daily, with vitamin C source
Expect Hb rise of 10 g/L in 4 weeks
Treat for 3 months total to refill stores
Low ferritin + high RDW + microcytosis = iron deficiency until proven otherwise
explanation.pearls: Mild thrombocytosis is common in iron deficiency and is not lymphoproliferative; it resolves with iron repletion.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Iron deficiency
RACGP Red Book - Preventive activities in children

stem: An 18-month-old girl is brought to her GP by her parents for ongoing management of atopic dermatitis diagnosed at 4 months. She has had multiple flares despite the parents using an over-the-counter aqueous cream as a soap substitute and 1% hydrocortisone applied 'when it gets really bad' for two to three days at a time, then stopped. She has dry skin with lichenified plaques in the antecubital and popliteal fossae, mild erythema on the cheeks, and excoriated patches on the dorsum of both hands. Her sleep is disrupted by scratching most nights. She had cow's milk protein allergy as an infant which has resolved, and her father has hay fever. There is no fever, no weeping or honey-crusting, no monomorphic vesicles, no lymphadenopathy, and her growth and development are normal. The parents are worried about 'steroid damage' and ask whether they can avoid topical steroids altogether.

data_table.Examination: Generalised xerosis. Lichenified flexural plaques. Excoriated dorsa of hands. No pustules, no monomorphic vesicles, no honey-crust. Weight on 60th centile.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate maintenance regimen?

options.text: Stop all topical steroids and use a thick paraffin-based emollient three to four times daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Emollients alone do not control established eczema with lichenification; appropriate topical corticosteroid use is safe and effective in children when matched to site and severity (RCH CPG Eczema).

options.text: Thick paraffin-based emollient at least twice daily, methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment once daily to flexural and trunk eczema until clear then twice weekly, and hydrocortisone 1% ointment to the face until clear
options.rationale: Site-matched topical corticosteroid potency (mild for face, moderate such as methylprednisolone aceponate for trunk/flexures) plus generous emollient is standard. Once-daily TCS until clear and then twice-weekly proactive application reduces flares (RCH CPG Eczema; TG Dermatology).

options.text: Betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% ointment twice daily to face and body until clear, then stop completely
options.rationale: Potent corticosteroids should not be used on the face in young children; an intermittent boom-bust strategy without proactive maintenance leads to repeated flares (TG Dermatology).

options.text: Refer for systemic ciclosporin given the chronicity of disease
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe, refractory eczema after optimised topical therapy; she has not had an adequate trial of site-matched TCS plus emollient (RCH CPG).

options.text: Daily bleach baths and oral cefalexin for two weeks as empirical antimicrobial maintenance
options.rationale: Routine oral antibiotics do not improve non-infected eczema and drive resistance; bleach baths may help colonisation but are not maintenance monotherapy (TG Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Atopic dermatitis maintenance combines liberal emollients with site-matched topical corticosteroids (mild on face/genitals, moderate on body/limbs, potent only with specialist input on thickened plaques) and proactive twice-weekly application to flare-prone sites once skin is clear.
key_points: Emollient at least twice daily, including soap substitute
Face: hydrocortisone 1% (mild); flexures/body: methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% (moderate)
Apply once daily until clear, then twice weekly proactively
Wet wraps over TCS for severe flares (short course)
Food allergy testing only if reproducible food-triggered flare
explanation.pearls: Aqueous cream contains sodium lauryl sulfate and is no longer recommended as a leave-on emollient; it can drive flares.

subtopic: Eczema flare with painful clustered vesicles
stem: A 3-year-old boy with longstanding moderate atopic dermatitis is brought to ED by his mother with a 36-hour history of a rapidly worsening rash on his face and neck, fever to 38.7 degC, and poor oral intake. He has been using methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment twice weekly proactively and an emollient four times daily, with good control until this week. There has been no overseas travel and his immunisations are up to date including MMR and varicella. His maternal grandfather had a cold sore on his lip when he kissed the patient three days ago. On examination he is miserable, drinking small sips, with grouped 2-3 mm punched-out monomorphic erosions and umbilicated vesicles in clusters on the left cheek, periorbital region and chin, some now coalescing. There is no honey-coloured crusting elsewhere, no ocular involvement on fluorescein staining, no neck stiffness, and no focal neurology. His pre-existing flexural eczema is otherwise stable. There are scattered satellite lesions on the right cheek and on his chest. He has had no contact with hand-foot-and-mouth disease at day care, no prior episodes of this kind, and his last wet nappy was 8 hours ago. There is no fluorescein uptake on the left cornea on bedside slit-lamp by the ED registrar, no preauricular lymphadenopathy, and his capillary refill is 2 seconds. He has been started on IV maintenance fluids and a swab from a deroofed vesicle has been couriered to the lab for HSV PCR.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% on room air, capillary refill 2 sec.
data_table.Examination: Clusters of monomorphic umbilicated 2-3 mm vesicles and punched-out erosions on left cheek, periorbital and chin. No corneal fluorescein uptake. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside HSV PCR swab from a deroofed vesicle sent. FBC, CRP, U&E pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Daily bleach baths and increase topical methylprednisolone aceponate to twice daily on the face
options.rationale: Potent TCS escalation on the face is not appropriate, particularly with concurrent HSV; bleach baths do not treat established HSV (RCH CPG).

options.text: Topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for five days and continue topical corticosteroids as usual
options.rationale: This is HSV, not impetigo; mupirocin will not treat the viral infection and continuing TCS over active HSV can worsen dissemination (TG Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg four times daily for seven days for impetiginised eczema
options.rationale: Honey-crusting and polymorphic pustules would suggest S aureus impetigo; the monomorphic umbilicated vesicle morphology and HSV contact indicate eczema herpeticum (RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit, start IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours, urgent ophthalmology review for the periorbital lesions, withhold topical corticosteroids on the affected sites, and treat as eczema herpeticum
options.rationale: Monomorphic umbilicated vesicles, punched-out erosions, fever, an HSV contact and rapid progression in an atopic child are eczema herpeticum. Periorbital involvement risks HSV keratitis, mandating admission, IV aciclovir and ophthalmology review (RCH CPG Eczema herpeticum).

options.text: Discharge home with oral valaciclovir and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Periorbital involvement, fever and poor oral intake in a 3-year-old with eczema herpeticum warrant admission for IV therapy and ophthalmology review (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Eczema herpeticum is disseminated HSV in atopic skin, presenting with monomorphic 2-3 mm umbilicated vesicles, punched-out erosions, systemic features and an HSV contact. Periocular disease threatens the cornea and mandates IV aciclovir plus ophthalmology review.
key_points: Monomorphic umbilicated vesicles = HSV (eczema herpeticum)
Polymorphic pustules with honey-crust = S aureus impetiginised eczema
Periocular HSV = admit, IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h, ophthalmology
Withhold TCS on actively infected sites until controlled
Co-infection with S aureus is common; cover if features suggest
explanation.pearls: If the morphology is monomorphic, think herpetic. If you see punched-out erosions in an atopic child, treat as HSV until proven otherwise.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Eczema herpeticum

subtopic: Disimpaction and maintenance in functional constipation
stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with her parents because of soiling at kindergarten and infrequent painful stools. Over the last 4 months she has opened her bowels twice a week, passing a very large hard stool that blocks the toilet, followed by small smears of loose stool in her underwear most days. Her parents thought the smears were diarrhoea and have been giving her smaller volumes of fluid. There is no blood in the stool, no vomiting, no fever, no weight loss; her growth is tracking the 50th centile. She passed meconium at 18 hours of age and toilet trained at 2.5 years. There is no family history of inflammatory bowel disease, coeliac disease or Hirschsprung disease. On examination she is well, abdomen is soft with a palpable non-tender faecal mass in the left iliac fossa, anus is normally placed with no fissure and no sacral dimple, lower limb tone, power and reflexes are normal.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, palpable faecal mass LIF. Normal anal position. No sacral dimple. Normal lower limb neurology. Growth on 50th centile.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Reassure the parents that her stool frequency is within normal limits and increase dietary fibre and fluid only
options.rationale: Overflow soiling on a background of large painful stools and a palpable faecal mass indicates impaction; dietary measures alone will not clear an impaction (RCH CPG Constipation).

options.text: Loperamide twice daily to control the loose smearing stool
options.rationale: The loose smearing is overflow around an impaction; antimotility agents worsen impaction (RCH CPG).

options.text: Oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes (Movicol Junior) escalating disimpaction regimen over 3-7 days, followed by daily maintenance dose, plus toileting routine and dietary advice
options.rationale: Functional constipation with faecal impaction and overflow soiling is managed with oral macrogol disimpaction (escalating dose over up to 7 days) followed by maintenance for at least 3 months, alongside behavioural toileting and dietary advice (RCH CPG Constipation; TG Gastrointestinal).

options.text: Refer for rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease before starting any laxative
options.rationale: Meconium passage was within 48 hours, growth is normal, and there is no abdominal distension or red flag; functional constipation is the working diagnosis and treatment should not be delayed (RCH CPG).

options.text: Phosphate enema in the GP rooms then discharge with senna only
options.rationale: Oral macrogol disimpaction is first-line in Australia; phosphate enemas carry risk of hyperphosphataemia in young children and are reserved for failed oral disimpaction (RCH CPG; AMH).

explanation.summary: Functional constipation with overflow soiling is treated with oral macrogol disimpaction followed by maintenance for at least 3 months, with behavioural toileting after meals (gastrocolic reflex), adequate fluid, fibre and physical activity.
key_points: Overflow incontinence is NOT diarrhoea; do not treat with antimotility agents
Macrogol 3350 with electrolytes is first-line for disimpaction and maintenance
Disimpaction escalation over 3-7 days, then maintenance dose
Maintain for at least 3 months and only wean once 6+ painless soft stools/week
Red flags: delayed meconium >48 h, FTT, abdominal distension, abnormal anus/neurology
explanation.pearls: Tell parents the smearing will get worse before it gets better during disimpaction; warn them so they don't stop the macrogol.

subtopic: Red flag features for organic cause of constipation in an infant
stem: A 7-week-old boy is brought to ED by his parents because he has not opened his bowels for 6 days and has had increasing abdominal distension and two episodes of bilious vomiting overnight. He was born at 39 weeks by normal vaginal delivery, weighing 3.5 kg. The mother reports that he did not pass meconium until day 3 of life and the midwife had needed to perform a rectal stimulation. He is exclusively formula-fed at 90 mL every 3 hours but has been taking less today. His weight today is 4.2 kg (birth weight 3.5 kg). When the registrar performs a careful digital rectal examination there is an explosive release of foul gas and liquid stool. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. There is no fever. The anus is normally positioned, the sacrum is intact and lower limb neurology is normal. The plain abdominal X-ray shows dilated bowel loops with a paucity of rectal gas. There is no maternal history of opioid or recreational drug use in pregnancy. Antenatal scans were unremarkable and there was no polyhydramnios. The newborn screening heel-prick was reported as normal including immunoreactive trypsinogen. He has no associated dysmorphism, no abnormal facies and no cardiac murmur. Calorie intake by parental report is approximately 130 mL/kg/day of standard infant formula. The surgical registrar has been contacted and an IV cannula sited; nasogastric tube is on standby for decompression while imaging is reviewed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 98%, capillary refill 3 sec.
data_table.Examination: Distended abdomen, tympanic, ribbon-like rectum on PR, explosive release on PR. Anus normally sited. No sacral dimple. Weight gain inadequate (only 700 g over 7 weeks).
data_table.Investigations: AXR: dilated bowel loops with paucity of rectal gas. FBC, U&E, VBG sent.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Functional constipation; commence oral macrogol disimpaction and discharge with GP review
options.rationale: Delayed meconium passage >48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting and explosive PR are red flags for an organic cause; discharge without further investigation is unsafe (RCH CPG Constipation).

options.text: Anal stenosis; perform serial anal dilatation in clinic
options.rationale: Anal stenosis presents with a narrow caudal anus on examination; here the anus is normally sited and the obstruction is functional (aganglionic segment), not anatomical at the anus (RCH CPG).

options.text: Cow's milk protein allergy; trial extensively hydrolysed formula and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: CMPA causes loose stool, blood-streaked stool or eczema/dermatitis rather than this picture of delayed meconium and obstruction (RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG Cow's milk protein allergy

options.text: Hirschsprung disease; refer for surgical review and arrange suction rectal biopsy
options.rationale: Delayed meconium >48 hours, abdominal distension, faltering growth, ribbon stool with explosive release on PR and bilious vomiting are classic for Hirschsprung disease. Suction rectal biopsy demonstrating aganglionosis is the diagnostic gold standard (RCH CPG; RACS).

options.text: Hypothyroidism; check TSH and free T4 before any further workup
options.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened on the newborn heel-prick (Guthrie) and typically causes prolonged jaundice and lethargy; it does not produce delayed meconium with explosive rectal release (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Red flags that mandate investigation for an organic cause of constipation in infancy include delayed meconium >48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, ribbon stool, abnormal anus/sacrum/neurology and explosive release on PR. The leading diagnosis is Hirschsprung disease; suction rectal biopsy is diagnostic.
key_points: Delayed meconium >48 hours = think Hirschsprung
Explosive release on PR is a classic clue
Suction rectal biopsy is the diagnostic test
Total colonic Hirschsprung can be associated with Down syndrome
Bilious vomiting in an infant is surgical until proven otherwise
explanation.pearls: Always ask 'when did meconium pass?' and 'did the midwife need to do anything to get it out?' in any constipated infant.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Bilious vomiting

subtopic: Assessment of inadequate weight gain in a young child
stem: A 10-month-old boy is reviewed by his GP at the routine immunisation visit. His weight today is 7.6 kg. He was born at 38+5 weighing 3.4 kg (50th centile) and tracked the 50th centile through to 6 months when he weighed 7.5 kg. He is breastfed on demand approximately 4 times a day and was started on solids at 6 months. His mother reports he is fussy with solids and refuses most foods other than rice porridge. He has no vomiting, no diarrhoea, no blood in the stool, no recurrent infections, no oral thrush and is developmentally appropriate. Family height: mother 168 cm, father 180 cm. He has a normal cardiovascular and respiratory examination, no lymphadenopathy, no organomegaly, no dysmorphic features. His length is on the 25th centile and head circumference on the 50th centile. His parents both work full-time and a grandparent provides daytime care; mealtime grazing on biscuits between meals is common. He has had no antenatal concerns, no NICU admission, and his newborn examination was unremarkable. He has not been screened for coeliac serology, thyroid function or iron studies. His mother breastfeeds approximately 4 times in 24 hours, including overnight, and reports she does not see him take full feeds; he is often distracted at the breast. The mid-parental height projects to the 50th centile.

data_table.Examination: Alert, interactive. No dysmorphism. No oedema. Normal cardiorespiratory exam. No organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Growth chart: weight has dropped from 50th to <10th centile over 4 months; length on 25th; HC on 50th. No investigations done yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial step?

options.text: Start an appetite stimulant such as cyproheptadine and review in one month
options.rationale: Pharmacological appetite stimulation is not first-line in young children; address feeding behaviour, energy density of solids, and mealtime environment first (RCH CPG; AMH Children's).

options.text: Admit to hospital for nasogastric feeding and observed weight gain
options.rationale: Hospital admission is reserved for severe malnutrition, safeguarding concerns or failed community management. Community-based assessment with dietitian and family support is first-line (RCH CPG).

options.text: Order a broad screen including coeliac serology, TFTs, sweat test and full metabolic screen at first presentation
options.rationale: Investigations are guided by history and examination; only 1-2% of faltering growth has a non-nutritional organic cause. A broad untargeted screen is low-yield (RCH CPG).

options.text: Refer immediately to child protection services on suspicion of neglect
options.rationale: Safeguarding is considered if the history is inconsistent or there are other concerns; first-line assessment is feeding history and dietary advice (RCH CPG).

options.text: Take a detailed feeding history (24-hour recall, feed volumes, solid intake, mealtime environment) and observe a feed before ordering any investigations
options.rationale: Faltering growth is most commonly due to inadequate intake. The first step is a detailed feeding and dietary history with a feed observation, before targeted investigations are considered. Anthropometry should plot weight, length and head circumference and check for centile crossing (RCH CPG Faltering growth).

explanation.summary: Faltering growth is defined by sustained drop across centiles (typically >=2 major centiles on the weight chart). The three buckets are: inadequate intake (commonest), malabsorption, and increased metabolic demand. Always observe a feed and take a 24-hour recall before broad investigation.
key_points: Plot weight, length and head circumference; check for centile crossing
Head sparing in early malnutrition (weight drops, length next, HC last)
Inadequate intake is the commonest cause
Targeted investigations only after history/examination flag a cause
Multidisciplinary management (dietitian, MCHN, paediatrician) for sustained faltering
explanation.pearls: A single low weight is not faltering growth; you need the trajectory. Always plot the chart yourself.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Faltering growth
RACGP Red Book - Preventive activities in children

subtopic: Stimulant initiation in school-aged inattention and hyperactivity
stem: An 8-year-old boy is reviewed by a paediatrician at the request of his GP and school. For the last 18 months he has had difficulty sustaining attention to schoolwork, frequently leaves his seat, interrupts conversations, loses his pencil case daily and forgets multi-step instructions. His Year 3 teacher and his swimming coach have completed Conners questionnaires which were both above clinical cut-offs. His mother reports similar behaviours at home, including weekends and school holidays, since pre-school. He has had no academic regression, has age-appropriate reading and numeracy, has friends but tends to fall out with them over impulsive comments, and has no features of low mood, anxiety, tics, autism or psychosis. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death, long QT or cardiomyopathy. On examination he is well, height 50th centile, weight 60th centile, BP 100/64, HR 86 with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. His mother has separately disclosed that she also suspects ADHD in herself and is interested in being assessed after her son's care is settled. There is no history of head injury, no perinatal hypoxia, no seizures and no language delay. He has no symptoms of restless legs, no snoring on direct questioning, and his sleep onset latency is appropriate. There is no anxiety disorder, no oppositional defiant features beyond developmental norms, and no learning disability identified on school psychometric assessment. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Examination: No dysmorphism. Normal cardiovascular and respiratory exam. No tics observed.
data_table.Investigations: Conners ratings above cut-off from two informants. ECG: sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms, no LVH, no pre-excitation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of restricted elimination diet for 6 weeks before pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Restrictive diets are not recommended as a primary intervention; behavioural and environmental supports plus stimulants where indicated are first-line (RACGP; TG Psychotropic).

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 0.5 mg/kg/day as first-line pharmacotherapy without a stimulant trial
options.rationale: Stimulants (methylphenidate or dexamphetamine) are first-line in children with ADHD without significant contraindications; atomoxetine is reserved for stimulant intolerance, tics, anxiety or risk of diversion (AADPA ADHD Guideline 2022).

options.text: Commence immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg twice daily under paediatrician TGA authorisation, with parent and school behavioural strategies, and monitor height, weight, BP, HR and side effects
options.rationale: He meets DSM-5 ADHD criteria (>=6 months, two settings, functional impairment). Methylphenidate is first-line; in Australia, stimulants require state authority/TGA pathway via a paediatrician. Behavioural interventions run in parallel and monitoring includes growth, BP, HR and mental state (AADPA 2022; RACGP).

options.text: Watchful waiting for a further 12 months with a school behavioural plan only
options.rationale: Symptoms have been present >6 months with functional impairment in two settings; further delay risks academic and social harm. Behavioural strategies are necessary but not sufficient (AADPA 2022).

options.text: Refer for cardiology review and stress echocardiogram before any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Routine cardiology review is not required when history, examination and resting ECG are unremarkable; only red flags (family sudden death, syncope on exertion, abnormal cardiac exam or ECG) mandate cardiology input (AADPA 2022).

explanation.summary: ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 symptom counts present for >=6 months, in >=2 settings, causing functional impairment, with onset before age 12. First-line pharmacotherapy is a stimulant (methylphenidate or dexamphetamine) under paediatrician supervision and state authority/TGA authorisation, alongside behavioural and educational supports.
key_points: DSM-5: >=6 inattentive and/or >=6 hyperactive-impulsive symptoms (<17 yo)
Two settings, >=6 months, onset <12 years, functional impairment
Pre-stimulant: cardiac history, family SCD history, exam, ECG
Monitor height, weight, BP, HR, mood, sleep, appetite
Stimulants are S8 in Australia and require state authority
explanation.pearls: School-only symptoms suggest a learning disorder or environment problem; home-only symptoms suggest parenting or attachment. ADHD is across settings.

sources.label: AADPA Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline 2022

subtopic: Developmental concern at 18 months with social communication features
stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his parents for his routine check. His mother has noticed that he does not respond to his name, rarely makes eye contact, does not point to share interest, does not wave, and has only 3 single words used inconsistently. He prefers to line up his cars and becomes distressed when the line is disturbed. He had normal hearing screening at birth, no birth complications, no significant medical illnesses, and is up to date with immunisations. His gross motor skills are typical (walks well, climbs stairs holding the rail). His paternal cousin has an autism diagnosis. Examination is unremarkable: no dysmorphic features, no neurocutaneous stigmata, no organomegaly. His M-CHAT-R/F at this visit shows a score of 8 (high risk threshold >=8). There has been no language regression and no loss of previously acquired skills. He had a febrile illness with rash at 11 months that resolved within 48 hours. He attends a long day care 2 days per week and the educators have raised similar concerns to the parents about his lack of response to his name and limited interaction with peers, often playing in parallel rather than reciprocally. He has had no episodes consistent with seizures, no head injuries and no recurrent ear infections. There is no maternal alcohol use during pregnancy. The parents speak English at home.

data_table.Vital signs: Afebrile, growth on 50th centile across the board.
data_table.Examination: No dysmorphism. No cafe-au-lait macules. No organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: M-CHAT-R/F score 8 (high risk). Newborn hearing screen normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure and review at the 2-year check, as language development is variable
options.rationale: He has multiple core features of autism spectrum disorder (joint attention deficits, social communication deficits, restricted/repetitive interests) plus an M-CHAT-R/F at high-risk threshold; reassurance delays access to early intervention (RACGP; Autism CRC National Guideline).

options.text: Refer to a multidisciplinary autism assessment team (paediatrician + speech pathology + psychology), arrange a formal audiology assessment, and initiate an NDIS Early Childhood early intervention referral while awaiting diagnosis
options.rationale: M-CHAT-R/F >=8 with concerning features warrants referral for a multidisciplinary autism assessment, formal audiology to exclude hearing loss as a contributor, and early access to NDIS Early Childhood early intervention (available before formal diagnosis if developmental delay is documented) (Autism CRC National Guideline; NDIS Early Childhood).

options.text: Order chromosomal microarray and Fragile X testing in the GP rooms today and review when results return
options.rationale: Genetic testing (CMA and Fragile X) is appropriate as part of the workup of confirmed developmental delay, but is typically arranged by the paediatrician alongside the multidisciplinary assessment, not as a standalone first GP step (Autism CRC).

options.text: Start risperidone for irritability and stereotyped behaviour
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy in ASD is reserved for specific severe behavioural symptoms after specialist assessment; it is not first-line at presentation and not appropriate at age 18 months (TG Psychotropic).

options.text: Refer only to speech pathology and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Speech pathology alone misses the multidimensional nature of ASD; multidisciplinary assessment with audiology and NDIS early intervention is needed (Autism CRC).

explanation.summary: ASD diagnostic criteria require persistent deficits in social communication AND restricted/repetitive behaviours, present from early development, causing functional impairment. M-CHAT-R/F is the validated 18-24 month screen. NDIS Early Childhood provides early intervention before formal diagnosis if developmental delay is documented.
key_points: Red flags at 18 mo: no response to name, no joint attention, no pointing to share, language regression
M-CHAT-R/F: low risk 0-2, medium 3-7 (do follow-up), high >=8 (refer)
Always check hearing in any speech delay
Multidisciplinary assessment: paediatrician + speech path + psychology
NDIS Early Childhood is available from before diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Joint attention (sharing interest with another person via gaze or pointing) is the single most predictive early autism feature.

sources.label: Autism CRC National Guideline for the Assessment and Diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorders 2018

stem: A 15-year-old girl presents to her GP with her mother, who has found superficial linear cuts on her daughter's forearm. The patient initially declines to speak in front of her mother, so the GP sees her alone after confidentiality is explained. Over a confidential HEEADSSS interview she describes 4 months of low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep, fatigue, poor concentration affecting her grades, weight loss of 3 kg, and feelings of hopelessness. She has been cutting her forearm with a razor weekly for 6 weeks for emotional relief. She reports passive thoughts that she would rather not be alive, but denies a current plan, intent, access to means, or previous attempts. There is no substance use, she is sexually inexperienced, and there is no disclosure of abuse. Her older brother has bipolar disorder. She agrees that her mother can be involved in safety planning. Examination shows superficial linear forearm cuts, no active bleeding, no concerning weight loss on growth chart, BMI 19. She has had no inpatient medical or psychiatric admissions previously. She has supportive friendships at school but has withdrawn from her netball team over the past 2 months. There is no history of trauma, sexual abuse or domestic violence on confidential enquiry, and no eating disorder behaviours (no restriction, no binge-purge, no body image preoccupation). She is not on any regular medications. Her mother has a history of depression treated with sertraline some years ago. She agrees to a follow-up plan and accepts referral to local youth mental health services.

data_table.Examination: Multiple superficial linear forearm cuts. Affect flat. Speech soft but coherent. No psychotic features. No infection.
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated at the initial visit.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Maintain confidentiality from the parents in all circumstances given she is a Gillick-competent minor
options.rationale: Confidentiality has limits when there is risk of harm; involving a supportive parent in safety planning improves outcomes and should be sought with the adolescent's consent, while respecting confidentiality on non-safety matters (RACGP; headspace).

options.text: Start fluoxetine 20 mg daily today with no psychological referral, and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Psychological therapy is first-line for mild-moderate adolescent depression; fluoxetine (if used) should be started at 10 mg with close monitoring for activation/suicidality in the first 4 weeks, alongside psychological care (TG Psychotropic; RANZCP).

options.text: Admit involuntarily to an adolescent inpatient mental health unit
options.rationale: Involuntary admission is reserved for acute risk with intent and plan or inability to maintain safety. With passive ideation, no plan or intent, family support, and willingness to engage, community management with safety planning is appropriate (RANZCP).

options.text: Develop a written safety plan with the patient (including means restriction, identified supports, crisis line numbers, headspace referral), involve her mother as a key support with the patient's consent, arrange GP follow-up within 1 week, and refer to local CAMHS for assessment for moderate-severe depression
options.rationale: Adolescent depression with self-harm and passive suicidal ideation requires a documented safety plan with means restriction, family involvement (with consent where possible), early review, and specialist mental health referral. headspace and CAMHS provide tiered care. Pharmacotherapy is considered when psychological therapy is insufficient or symptoms are moderate-severe and persistent (RANZCP; headspace).

options.text: Reassure that self-harm without suicidal intent is a normal phase of adolescence and review in 3 months
options.rationale: Self-harm with persistent low mood and passive suicidal ideation is a marker of mental illness and elevated future suicide risk; it requires structured assessment and intervention (RANZCP; headspace).

explanation.summary: Adolescent depression with self-harm requires a HEEADSSS-based assessment, structured suicide risk assessment, a written safety plan, family involvement with consent, and tiered referral (GP -> headspace -> CAMHS). Fluoxetine is the first-line SSRI for adolescents with moderate-severe depression, started low with early review for activation.
key_points: HEEADSSS: Home, Education/Employment, Eating, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/self-harm, Safety
Safety plan: warning signs, coping, distractions, social supports, professional contacts, means restriction
Fluoxetine is the SSRI with the best evidence in adolescent depression
Black-box: monitor closely for activation/suicidality first 4 weeks of SSRI
headspace is the national youth mental health service (12-25 yo)
explanation.pearls: Means restriction (lock medicines, remove blades) is the single highest-impact element of a safety plan.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines - Mood disorders

subtopic: Severely underweight adolescent with food restriction
stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to ED by her parents after collapsing on standing. Over 9 months her weight has dropped from 58 kg to 38 kg; her height is 165 cm (BMI 14.0). She restricts intake to <600 kcal/day, exercises for 2-3 hours daily and induces vomiting after any meal she perceives as 'too much'. She has not menstruated for 5 months. She insists she is fat. There has been no binge eating, no laxative use, no diuretic use, no substance use, no overt depressive symptoms beyond food preoccupation, no suicidal ideation. Examination: alert, cooperative, dry mucous membranes, lanugo on the back, BP 84/52 supine dropping to 70/40 on standing with HR 38 rising to 64, T 35.4 degC, capillary refill 3 sec, peripherally cool. ECG shows sinus bradycardia, QTc 470 ms. Bedside investigations: Na 134, K 3.0, phosphate 0.7, magnesium 0.6, glucose 3.4, urea 6.2, creatinine 64, ALT 110. She had a normal birth and early development, sat at 6 months, walked at 13 months, achieved menarche at 12, and was previously academically high-performing. She denies laxative or diuretic use. She has no chest pain, no palpitations on exertion, no syncope episodes other than this collapse. She has no haematemesis, no diarrhoea, no constipation other than infrequent stool, and no abdominal pain. Family history: maternal aunt with bulimia nervosa, no thyroid disease, no diabetes, no coeliac disease. She has not previously engaged with any mental health service. Her parents are both present at the bedside and engaged in care.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/52 supine -> 70/40 standing, HR 38 -> 64 standing, T 35.4 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: BMI 14.0. Lanugo. Cool peripheries. Capillary refill 3 sec. No focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Na 134, K 3.0, PO4 0.7 mmol/L, Mg 0.6 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, ALT 110 U/L. ECG: sinus bradycardia 38, QTc 470 ms.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Discharge home with outpatient dietitian and weekly GP review
options.rationale: She meets multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria (HR <45, postural BP drop >20/10 with HR rise >30, T <35.5 degC, QTc prolongation, hypophosphataemia, hypoglycaemia); inpatient admission is mandatory (RANZCP; ANZAED; MARSIPAN-AU).
source_refs: RANZCP Eating Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nightly for body image distortion and discharge home
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive in eating disorders, not a substitute for medical stabilisation and family-based treatment, and discharge home would be unsafe (RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Eating Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Admit and commence high-calorie refeeding at 2000 kcal/day from day 1 to rapidly restore weight
options.rationale: Aggressive refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome (fluid overload, severe hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia, Wernicke encephalopathy); refeeding should start cautiously and titrate up (MARSIPAN-AU).

options.text: Admit to a psychiatric ward only and defer medical management until psychiatric review the following day
options.rationale: She is medically unstable with electrolyte derangement and bradycardia; medical stabilisation precedes or runs alongside psychiatric admission (MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Eating Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Admit to a paediatric medical bed, cautious refeeding starting at approximately 5-10 kcal/kg/day with daily phosphate, potassium, magnesium and glucose monitoring, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring, and engage adolescent mental health for family-based treatment planning
options.rationale: She has severe anorexia nervosa with refeeding syndrome risk (low PO4, low Mg, low K, low glucose). MARSIPAN-AU mandates medical admission with cautious refeeding, electrolyte and glucose monitoring, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia, and FBT engagement (MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).
source_refs: RANZCP Eating Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: MARSIPAN-AU high-risk indicators for anorexia nervosa admission include BMI <14 (or <13 in adolescents), HR <45, postural BP fall >20/10 with HR rise >30, T <35.5 degC, QTc >450 ms, hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypoglycaemia and acute medical instability. Refeeding starts low (5-10 kcal/kg/day) with daily electrolytes, prophylactic phosphate and thiamine, cardiac monitoring.
key_points: Refeeding syndrome: low PO4, K, Mg; fluid shifts; arrhythmia; Wernicke
Start refeeding cautiously - 'start low, go slow' especially in severe AN
Prophylactic thiamine 100 mg before any glucose-containing fluids
Family-Based Treatment (FBT/Maudsley) is first-line psychotherapy for adolescent AN
Monitor QTc daily during refeeding
explanation.pearls: The first 72 hours of refeeding kill people, not the starvation. Phosphate is the canary.

sources.label: MARSIPAN-AU - Management of Really Sick Patients with Anorexia Nervosa
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline - Eating Disorders

subtopic: Imaging after a second febrile UTI in a young girl
stem: A 3-year-old girl is reviewed in clinic two weeks after her second culture-proven E coli UTI with fever to 39 degC, both treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole as per local sensitivities. The first UTI was 4 months ago. She has had ongoing functional constipation for which her parents administer macrogol with good effect. She is otherwise well, growth on the 60th centile, with normal developmental milestones and an unremarkable antenatal renal ultrasound. There is no family history of renal disease or VUR. Her renal ultrasound after this admission shows mildly dilated left renal pelvis (AP diameter 8 mm), no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex system, and normal bladder wall thickness with no post-void residual.

data_table.Examination: Well, abdomen soft, no flank tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Renal USS: left renal pelvis AP 8 mm, no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex. Urine MCS clear since treatment.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Arrange a DMSA scan 4-6 months after the most recent infection to assess for renal cortical scarring
options.rationale: RCH CPG and KHA-CARI recommend DMSA scarring assessment after recurrent febrile UTI in young children, performed 4-6 months after the acute infection to allow acute inflammation to settle and detect persistent scarring. MCUG is reserved for cases with hydroureter, abnormal USS or recurrence after DMSA (RCH CPG UTI; KHA-CARI).

options.text: Arrange an MCUG within the next week
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive (catheterisation, ionising radiation) and not first-line after recurrent UTI in Australia; DMSA is preferred to detect scarring and stratify further imaging (RCH CPG; KHA-CARI).

options.text: Commence trimethoprim 2 mg/kg nightly as long-term prophylaxis without further imaging
options.rationale: Antibiotic prophylaxis is considered after imaging stratifies the risk (high-grade VUR, significant scarring); it is not started empirically without imaging. Concurrent treatment of constipation is also key (RCH CPG; TG Antibiotic).

options.text: Refer for cystoscopy to evaluate the bladder
options.rationale: Cystoscopy is not indicated in straightforward recurrent UTI without anatomical concerns on USS; it has no role in routine paediatric UTI workup (RCH CPG).

options.text: Repeat the renal ultrasound in 12 months and take no further action
options.rationale: With two febrile UTIs, deferred imaging misses scarring and treatable contributors; current Australian guidance recommends DMSA 4-6 months post-infection (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: After recurrent febrile UTI in young children, RCH and KHA-CARI recommend renal ultrasound acutely and DMSA 4-6 months later to assess for cortical scarring. MCUG is targeted (abnormal USS, abnormal DMSA, or further breakthroughs). Constipation contributes substantially to UTI recurrence and must be optimised.
key_points: USS during/after first febrile UTI in any child <2 yo
DMSA 4-6 months after recurrent febrile UTI
MCUG if hydroureter, dilated collecting system, or further breakthrough
Treat constipation - reduces UTI recurrence
Prophylaxis individualised: high-grade VUR, severe scarring, breakthrough UTI
explanation.pearls: If you can fix the constipation you fix the UTI in many of these children.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Urinary tract infection

subtopic: Routine surveillance review at one year of age
stem: A 12-month-old girl attends her routine GP visit. Her mother reports she is well, is feeding family foods three times daily with breastfeeds on demand, sleeps through the night, is pulling to stand, cruising furniture, says 'mama' and 'dada' meaningfully and waves bye-bye. She has had no significant illnesses. Her growth is tracking the 50th centile. Newborn hearing screen was clear and she has had all immunisations to date. She lives at home with both parents and an older sibling. Family history is unremarkable. The mother reports she is well herself and her postnatal Edinburgh score at 6 months was reassuring. There is a working smoke alarm in the house and the child is in a forward-facing car seat with harness. The family attends a community dentist annually. There is no household pet bite history, no household firearm and no swimming pool at home.

data_table.Examination: Alert, social smile, interactive. Growth on 50th centile. No dysmorphism. Normal cardiorespiratory examination.
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated routinely at this visit.

lead_in: Which combination is the correct routine 12-month immunisation cohort under the current Australian National Immunisation Program?

options.text: MMRV + Hep A + influenza
options.rationale: Varicella as MMRV is given at 18 months in the NIP, not 12 months. Hep A is funded for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in certain jurisdictions, not universally at 12 months (NCIRS).

options.text: DTPa-hepB-IPV-Hib + rotavirus + meningococcal B
options.rationale: These are primary series doses given at 2, 4 and 6 months; rotavirus has an upper age limit and is not given at 12 months (NCIRS).

options.text: MMR + meningococcal ACWY + pneumococcal conjugate (13vPCV)
options.rationale: The 12-month NIP schedule comprises MMR (live), meningococcal ACWY conjugate, and the third dose of pneumococcal conjugate (13vPCV). Varicella and Hepatitis A (in eligible groups) are given at 18 months (NCIRS Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: HPV + DTPa booster
options.rationale: HPV is given in adolescence and DTPa booster is at 18 months and 4 years; neither is at the 12-month visit (NCIRS).

options.text: BCG + oral polio
options.rationale: BCG is not routine on the NIP and oral polio is not used in Australia (IPV is given as part of combination vaccines) (NCIRS).

explanation.summary: The Australian NIP 12-month visit includes MMR (live), meningococcal ACWY, and pneumococcal conjugate (13vPCV). The 12-month well-child check also covers growth, milestones (gross/fine motor, language, social), nutrition, sleep, safety (car seats, drowning, choking, sun), dental (first visit by 12 months/eruption of first tooth), hearing/vision concerns and family wellbeing.
key_points: 12 months: MMR, MenACWY, 13vPCV
18 months: MMRV (varicella), DTPa booster, Hib
4 years: DTPa-IPV booster
Watch for milestone red flags at every visit
Family wellbeing: maternal mental health, smoke exposure, food security
explanation.pearls: Use every well-child visit to check on parental mental health - PND can present late, especially after the perinatal screen ends.

sources.label: RACGP Red Book - Preventive activities in children

subtopic: Live vaccine considerations in a household with immunocompromise
stem: A 17-month-old boy attends his GP for delayed 12-month immunisations because the family has been overseas. He is well, immunocompetent, with no chronic illness. His mother is on the bone marrow transplant register and has just commenced cyclophosphamide-based conditioning for an upcoming allogeneic transplant in 4 weeks; she lives in the same household. The boy's 4-year-old sister is also up to date. His mother is concerned about the safety of his vaccines given her own state and asks the GP for advice. His outstanding 12-month vaccines include MMR, meningococcal ACWY and the third dose of 13vPCV. He has had no recent live vaccines, no immunosuppressive therapy of his own, and no contact with active tuberculosis. The mother's transplant team has been in contact with the family GP and a written summary of household infection-control measures is being prepared. The mother has had her own pre-transplant influenza, pneumococcal and any catch-up inactivated vaccines administered as advised by haematology. The father is asymptomatic and fully vaccinated.

data_table.Examination: Well child, no rash, no respiratory or other illness.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding his catch-up immunisations?

options.text: Defer all of the child's vaccines until after the mother's transplant and engraftment
options.rationale: Deferring all vaccines leaves the immunocompromised mother more vulnerable to vaccine-preventable disease introduced into the home; the child should be fully immunised, including with most live vaccines (NCIRS).

options.text: Give MMR and varicella as scheduled and the inactivated vaccines as scheduled, and counsel that the child does not need to be isolated from his mother routinely; manage any post-varicella rash with isolation and oral aciclovir if vesicles develop
options.rationale: MMR and varicella vaccine viruses are not transmitted from immunocompetent vaccinees in routine contact (varicella vaccine virus transmission is rare and only from vesicular rash). Immunising household contacts of immunocompromised people is recommended. Rotavirus is the exception (faecal shedding) and would need hand hygiene precautions; he is too old for rotavirus anyway (NCIRS).

options.text: Replace MMR with an inactivated alternative because of household immunocompromise
options.rationale: There is no inactivated MMR alternative on the NIP, and MMR vaccine viruses are not transmitted from vaccinees to contacts; the standard MMR should be given (NCIRS).

options.text: Give the child oral typhoid (live) vaccine prophylactically because of expected hospital visits
options.rationale: Oral typhoid vaccine is not routinely indicated and is not part of NIP catch-up; this introduces a live oral vaccine inappropriately (NCIRS).

options.text: Give only inactivated vaccines and avoid all live vaccines until the mother is post-transplant and immune-reconstituted
options.rationale: MMR and varicella are not contraindicated in household contacts of immunocompromised people; the child remains at risk of vaccine-preventable disease and his mother is more vulnerable if he is unprotected (NCIRS; eviQ).

explanation.summary: Live vaccines are contraindicated IN the immunocompromised person but in general are SAFE for their household contacts (MMR not transmitted; varicella vaccine virus transmission rare and only via vesicular rash). Rotavirus is the practical exception due to faecal shedding - hand hygiene needed for 4-6 weeks. Influenza inactivated is encouraged for household contacts annually.
key_points: Live vaccines contraindicated IN immunocompromised individuals
Household contacts SHOULD be immunised, including MMR and varicella
Manage post-varicella rash with covering/isolation
Rotavirus faecal shedding - hand hygiene precaution for 4-6 weeks
Influenza (inactivated) annually for all household contacts
explanation.pearls: Cocooning protects the immunocompromised; under-immunising the household is the opposite of safe.

subtopic: Parental hesitancy about routine childhood vaccination
stem: A 5-month-old breastfed boy attends his GP with his father, who has read 'a lot online' and wants to delay the 6-month vaccines. The mother is supportive of vaccination and has asked the father to discuss it with the doctor. The father is most concerned that 'too many vaccines at once overload the immune system' and that aluminium adjuvants 'cause autism'. He has not refused completely; he describes himself as hesitant. The infant is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile, with no medical issues. He had his 2 and 4-month vaccines without adverse events. The father acknowledges the parents' rights and obligations under their state's framework but worries about social media accounts he follows.
lead_in: Which approach is most consistent with current Australian guidance on vaccine hesitancy counselling?

options.text: Refuse to provide any further GP care for the family until the father agrees to vaccination
options.rationale: Withholding general care is unethical and not supported by RACGP or NCIRS; trusted continuity is a key driver of eventual acceptance (RACGP; NCIRS Sharing Knowledge About Immunisation).

options.text: Use a motivational interviewing approach: explore the father's specific concerns, affirm his role as a parent, share tailored factual information addressing 'immune overload' and the disproven aluminium/autism link, give a clear recommendation to vaccinate today, and offer SKAI resources for him to read at home
options.rationale: NCIRS SKAI and RACGP recommend a motivational interviewing approach with presumptive language, exploration of concerns, tailored factual rebuttal, a clear clinician recommendation, and signposting to reputable resources. Adversarial debate is less effective than empathic engagement (NCIRS SKAI; RACGP).

options.text: Immediately notify child protection because vaccine refusal is medical neglect
options.rationale: Vaccine hesitancy in an otherwise well-cared-for child is not in itself medical neglect; mandatory reporting thresholds are not met by hesitancy alone (RACGP).

options.text: Offer a 'spaced-out' schedule giving one vaccine per visit as a compromise
options.rationale: Alternative schedules are not supported, prolong susceptibility, increase visits and pain, and reinforce the misconception of 'immune overload'. The standard schedule should be recommended (NCIRS).

options.text: Refer the family to the Family Court to compel vaccination given parental disagreement
options.rationale: Family Court orders are a last resort when parents are in deadlock and other approaches have failed; the initial step is shared GP-led discussion (RACGP).

explanation.summary: Vaccine hesitancy is best addressed with a presumptive opening, motivational interviewing, exploration of specific concerns, tailored factual rebuttal, an explicit clinician recommendation, and signposting to evidence-based resources (NCIRS SKAI). Maintain the therapeutic relationship; many hesitant parents accept vaccines over time with continuity.
key_points: Presumptive opening: 'Today we'll do the 6-month vaccines.'
Motivational interviewing: explore, affirm, reflect, summarise
Address specific concerns with specific evidence
Make an explicit recommendation - 'I recommend...'
SKAI website is the Australian go-to resource
explanation.pearls: Don't try to win the argument in one visit; you are building a relationship that often takes 2-3 conversations to land.

subtopic: Toddler with bow legs and biochemistry suggestive of metabolic bone disease
stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his mother because of bow legs and reluctance to walk. He was born at term, exclusively breastfed until 12 months without vitamin D supplementation, and has had limited sun exposure as the family wear concealing clothing for cultural reasons and live in a southern Australian city. His diet is otherwise low in dairy. There is no history of diarrhoea, fatty stools, recurrent infections, fractures or a family history of metabolic bone disease. He is on the 25th centile for length and 50th for weight. On examination he has frontal bossing, widening of the wrists, palpable rachitic rosary at the costochondral junctions, and genu varum. He has no carpopedal spasm and no seizure activity. His mother is otherwise well but reports myalgia and fatigue. The infant takes solids three times a day, including iron-fortified cereal, but very little dairy or oily fish. He has no diarrhoea, no steatorrhoea, no jaundice, and no history of premature birth or low birth weight. There is no family history of consanguinity, fragility fractures, or genetic bone disease. He has met expected developmental milestones for fine motor and language but has been slower to walk; he took his first independent steps at 16 months and is now unsteady, with frequent falls noticed by his mother. The wrist X-ray was ordered after the GP examined him today and the radiologist phoned through the metaphyseal findings.

data_table.Examination: Frontal bossing, wrist widening, rachitic rosary, genu varum. No tetany. Length 25th centile, weight 50th.
data_table.Investigations: 25-OH vitamin D 18 nmol/L, calcium 2.10 mmol/L (corrected), phosphate 0.9 mmol/L, ALP 920 U/L (markedly elevated), PTH 14.2 pmol/L (high), creatinine 24 micromol/L. Wrist X-ray: cupping and fraying of the distal radial and ulnar metaphyses.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Bisphosphonates to correct the bone deformity
options.rationale: Bisphosphonates have no role in nutritional rickets and could impair the mineralisation phase of healing; deformity usually corrects with vitamin D and calcium repletion (RCH CPG).

options.text: A single high-dose 'stoss' of cholecalciferol 600,000 IU orally with no follow-up
options.rationale: Stoss therapy is not preferred in young children due to risk of hypercalcaemia and the importance of follow-up biochemistry; daily replacement is safer and the standard approach in Australia (RCH CPG; APEG).

options.text: IV calcium gluconate today and admit for cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: IV calcium is reserved for symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, ECG changes); he is asymptomatic with mild hypocalcaemia, treated with oral calcium and cholecalciferol (RCH CPG).

options.text: Calcitriol (1,25-OH vitamin D) 0.25 microg daily as first-line
options.rationale: Calcitriol is reserved for vitamin D-dependent rickets, chronic kidney disease and hypoparathyroidism; nutritional deficiency is treated with cholecalciferol because the patient can still 1-alpha-hydroxylate (RCH CPG).

options.text: Cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU orally daily for 3 months, with calcium intake optimisation, sensible sun exposure advice, recheck 25-OH vitamin D, calcium, phosphate and ALP after treatment, and screen and treat his mother for deficiency
options.rationale: Confirmed nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets (low 25-OH vitamin D <50 nmol/L, raised ALP and PTH, radiographic changes) is treated with cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for 3 months in a toddler, plus 500 mg/day elemental calcium, with sun exposure counselling. Screen the breastfeeding mother because deficient mothers explain the infant's deficiency (RCH CPG Vitamin D; APEG/ASBMR consensus).
source_refs: APEG/ASBMR Australian and New Zealand consensus on vitamin D

explanation.summary: Nutritional vitamin D deficiency rickets is treated with cholecalciferol (1000-2000 IU/day for toddlers; 600 IU/day prevention) for 3 months, with calcium intake optimisation and sun exposure counselling. Always screen the breastfeeding mother. The 25-OH vitamin D threshold for deficiency is <50 nmol/L (severe <30 nmol/L).
key_points: 25-OH vitamin D: <30 severe deficient, <50 deficient, 50-75 insufficient
Raised ALP and PTH are early biochemical markers
X-ray: cupping and fraying of metaphyses (wrist, knees, ankles)
Treat with cholecalciferol (daily) - not calcitriol - for nutritional rickets
Screen the mother; vitamin D-deficient mothers explain vitamin D-deficient infants
explanation.pearls: If ALP is 'unbelievably high' in a toddler, think rickets before liver. ALP is the most sensitive biochemical sign.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Vitamin D deficiency
APEG/ASBMR Australian and New Zealand consensus on vitamin D
